subtopic_detail: Giant cell arteritis with visual symptoms
stem: A 74-year-old woman presents to your ED with 2 weeks of new bilateral temporal headache and 2 days of intermittent transient monocular vision loss in the right eye lasting a few minutes each episode. She has 3 weeks of jaw claudication. She was diagnosed with polymyalgia rheumatica 6 months ago and is on prednisolone 5 mg daily. She has no diplopia and no focal neurological deficit. Fundoscopy is normal.

data_table.Examination: Tender thickened right temporal artery; reduced pulsation; visual acuity 6/6 both eyes; pupils equal reactive; visual fields intact at this moment; tongue normal
data_table.Investigations: ESR 110 mm/hr, CRP 96 mg/L; Hb 108, platelets 412; LFTs ALP mildly raised at 142; UEC normal; CT brain normal; carotid Doppler arranged

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Administer intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days and arrange temporal artery biopsy within 14 days
options.rationale: Visual symptoms (amaurosis fugax) in suspected giant cell arteritis mandate immediate high-dose IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days to prevent permanent vision loss; temporal artery biopsy within 7-14 days remains positive despite steroids. (Source: Australian Rheumatology Association)
source_refs: Australian Rheumatology Association (ARA) GCA position statement

options.text: Defer steroid escalation until the temporal artery biopsy result is available to avoid masking diagnosis
options.rationale: Delaying steroids until biopsy risks permanent vision loss within hours to days; biopsy positivity remains adequate for 7-14 days after starting steroids. (Source: ARA)
source_refs: Australian Rheumatology Association (ARA) GCA position statement

options.text: Increase oral prednisolone to 40 mg daily and arrange temporal artery biopsy within 4 weeks at the next available slot
options.rationale: 40 mg oral prednisolone is appropriate for non-visual GCA, but visual symptoms warrant IV pulses for the first 3 days. (Source: ARA)
source_refs: Australian Rheumatology Association (ARA) GCA position statement

options.text: Commence aspirin 100 mg daily without altering current prednisolone, and refer to ophthalmology outpatients
options.rationale: Aspirin is adjunctive; alone it does not address the inflammatory mechanism and risk of permanent visual loss is unaddressed. (Source: ARA)
source_refs: Australian Rheumatology Association (ARA) GCA position statement

options.text: Arrange ultrasound of the temporal arteries as the sole confirmatory test and treat empirically based on the ultrasound alone
options.rationale: Temporal artery ultrasound (halo sign) has high specificity in expert hands but biopsy remains gold standard in Australia; do not delay treatment for either investigation. (Source: ARA)
source_refs: Australian Rheumatology Association (ARA) GCA position statement

explanation.summary: Giant cell arteritis is a large-vessel vasculitis of older adults presenting with new headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, and visual symptoms. Permanent visual loss occurs in 15-30 percent of untreated cases. Australian Rheumatology Association guidelines specify pulsed IV methylprednisolone for visual involvement, oral prednisolone 40-60 mg daily for non-visual disease, with temporal artery biopsy within 7-14 days retaining sensitivity. Adjunctive low-dose aspirin reduces cerebrovascular events. Long-term tocilizumab is steroid-sparing for relapsing GCA. PMR co-occurs in 40-50 percent.
key_points: Visual symptoms - IV methylpred pulses; non-visual - oral prednisolone 40-60 mg
Biopsy within 7-14 days of starting steroids remains diagnostic
ESR and CRP not specific; clinical diagnosis takes priority
Aspirin adjunct; tocilizumab steroid-sparing
explanation.pearls: Bilateral temporal artery biopsy increases yield by 5-10 percent over unilateral but a single 1-2 cm specimen is usually sufficient; skip lesions limit shorter biopsies, so insist on at least 1 cm of artery in the sample.

sources.label: Australian Rheumatology Association (ARA) GCA position statement
BMJ Best Practice: Giant Cell Arteritis

subtopic_detail: Acute adrenal crisis management in primary adrenal insufficiency
stem: A 58-year-old woman with known primary adrenal insufficiency presents to your ED with 24 hours of fever, vomiting, abdominal pain and lethargy. She has not been able to take her usual hydrocortisone 20 mg morning and 10 mg evening due to vomiting. She has type 1 diabetes, hypothyroidism, and a daughter with coeliac disease. She is hypotensive, drowsy and warm peripherally.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4, BP 78/45, HR 122, RR 24, SpO2 96 percent RA, GCS 13 (E3V4M6), BSL 3.2 mmol/L
data_table.Examination: Hyperpigmented skin and buccal mucosa, dry mucous membranes, soft non-peritonitic abdomen with mild epigastric tenderness; chest clear
data_table.Investigations: Na 124, K 5.8, glucose 3.2; creatinine 110 (baseline 70); CRP 84; lactate 3.2; FBC WCC 14.0; cortisol pending; urine ketones positive; CXR clear

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Withhold steroids until a random cortisol level is back to confirm the diagnosis before commencing therapy
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis is a clinical diagnosis; treatment must not await laboratory confirmation; mortality rises sharply with each hour of delay. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia)

options.text: Administer intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg stat plus intravenous 0.9 percent saline 1 L bolus and supplemental dextrose
options.rationale: Immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat (then 50 mg 6-hourly or 200 mg/24h infusion), 0.9 percent saline 1 L over 1 hour, and dextrose for hypoglycaemia are the cornerstones of adrenal crisis management. Hydrocortisone provides both glucocorticoid and modest mineralocorticoid activity. (Source: ESA; AMH)

options.text: Administer prednisolone 50 mg via nasogastric tube and commence intravenous saline once the prednisolone is absorbed
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone is unreliable when the patient is vomiting and has potentially impaired gut absorption; IV access is mandatory. (Source: ESA)

options.text: Administer intravenous dexamethasone 8 mg stat because it does not interfere with subsequent cortisol assay sampling
options.rationale: Dexamethasone is acceptable if hydrocortisone unavailable, but lacks mineralocorticoid activity needed for hyperkalaemia and hyponatraemia in primary adrenal crisis. (Source: ESA)

options.text: Commence noradrenaline infusion centrally before any glucocorticoid replacement to support blood pressure
options.rationale: Vasopressors are second-line if hypotension persists after steroid replacement and fluid resuscitation; replacing the missing hormone treats the cause. (Source: ESA)

explanation.summary: Acute adrenal crisis presents with hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia, and shock unresponsive to fluid alone. Mortality is around 1 in 200 per patient-year despite known diagnosis and stress-dose education. Management is immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat, followed by 200 mg/24h infusion or 50 mg every 6 hours, plus IV saline and dextrose. Investigate precipitant (infection commonest), continue stress doses until oral tolerated, then halve daily until baseline. All patients with adrenal insufficiency should have a Medic Alert bracelet and emergency hydrocortisone for self-injection.
key_points: Adrenal crisis: hypotension + hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + hypoglycaemia
Immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat, do not wait for cortisol result
Saline 1 L stat, dextrose for hypoglycaemia
Investigate precipitant (infection most common)
explanation.pearls: Patients with adrenal insufficiency on long-term hydrocortisone must double or triple their daily dose during acute illness or fever above 38 degrees, and switch to IM hydrocortisone 100 mg self-injection for any vomiting episode lasting more than 1 hour - failure to do so is the commonest precipitant of crisis.

stem: A 36-year-old woman presents to your GP clinic for migraine prevention. She has had migraine with aura for 12 years, currently 6 attacks per month each lasting 8-12 hours. She had reasonable benefit from propranolol 40 mg twice daily for 3 months (cutting frequency to 3 per month) but stopped 4 weeks ago after fainting once during a 5 km run and developing exertional bradycardia. She has well-controlled asthma on Symbicort. She is planning pregnancy in the next 12 months.

data_table.Examination: Normal neurological examination including fundi; no focal deficit
data_table.Investigations: Recent thyroid normal; ECG sinus bradycardia 52 bpm without QT prolongation; FBC normal; pregnancy test negative
data_table.Medications: Stopped propranolol 4 weeks ago; using sumatriptan 50 mg PRN

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next preventive treatment?

options.text: Restart propranolol at a reduced dose of 20 mg twice daily and recommend avoidance of strenuous exercise altogether
options.rationale: Propranolol is contraindicated by recurrent symptomatic bradycardia and risk in asthma; restart is not appropriate. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Commence amitriptyline 25 mg nocte with explicit anti-cholinergic side effect counselling before her planned conception
options.rationale: Amitriptyline is reasonable for migraine plus tension-type headache, but topiramate has stronger evidence as monotherapy; both require pregnancy planning discussion. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Commence sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily as an effective and well-tolerated long-term preventive option
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is contraindicated in women of childbearing potential because of teratogenicity (neural tube defects 1-2 percent and developmental delay) and requires the Pregnancy Prevention Programme; not appropriate when pregnancy is planned. (Source: AMH; TGA)

options.text: Commence topiramate 25 mg nocte with planned titration to 50 mg twice daily and pregnancy counselling
options.rationale: Topiramate is first-line preventive for migraine with aura, evidence-based for 50 percent or more reduction in frequency; teratogenicity (oral cleft, low birth weight) requires effective contraception and discontinuation 4-6 weeks before planned conception. (Source: AMH; eTG Neurology)

options.text: Commence verapamil 80 mg three times daily as an effective option for migraine with aura prevention
options.rationale: Verapamil is for cluster headache prophylaxis, not migraine; not indicated here. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Migraine prophylaxis is considered for 4 or more attacks per month or significant disability. First-line agents in Australia include propranolol (avoid in asthma, bradycardia), candesartan (good pregnancy profile), topiramate (teratogenic - oral cleft risk), amitriptyline, and sodium valproate (avoid in women of childbearing potential). Newer CGRP-monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab) are PBS-restricted to refractory cases with failed 3 prior preventives. Treatment is reviewed at 3 months. Acute attacks treated with triptans or NSAIDs.
key_points: Migraine prevention if 4 or more per month or disabling
First-line AU: propranolol, candesartan, topiramate, amitriptyline
Valproate banned in women of childbearing age (PPP TGA)
Topiramate teratogenic; counsel pre-conception
explanation.pearls: Candesartan 16 mg daily has emerging evidence as a first-line migraine preventive with the best pregnancy compatibility (taper before conception), suiting women planning pregnancy who cannot tolerate propranolol; effect onset is 6-8 weeks.

subtopic_detail: Hyperkalaemia with ECG changes and trimethoprim trigger
stem: A 67-year-old man presents to your ED with palpitations and weakness for 12 hours. He has stage 4 CKD (baseline eGFR 22), heart failure with reduced EF, and type 2 diabetes. He takes ramipril 10 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, frusemide 40 mg, and metformin. He was recently started on trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for a UTI. Triage ECG shows tall peaked T waves and widening QRS.

data_table.Examination: Mild peripheral oedema; chest with bibasal crackles
data_table.Investigations: K 7.2 mmol/L, Na 138, urea 18, creatinine 320 (baseline 220), bicarbonate 18, glucose 6.4; VBG pH 7.30; ECG: tall peaked T waves V2-V6, QRS 132 ms, no P waves visible

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate first step?

options.text: Administer intravenous insulin 10 units in 50 percent dextrose 50 mL to shift potassium intracellularly
options.rationale: Insulin and dextrose are appropriate but second after membrane stabilisation; insulin lowers K by 0.5-1.2 mmol/L over 30 minutes, acts within 15 minutes. (Source: KHA-CARI; AMH)

options.text: Administer salbutamol 10 mg nebulised over 10 minutes to shift potassium intracellularly via beta-2 receptors
options.rationale: Salbutamol shifts K but acts more slowly and unreliably than insulin; appropriate as additional shift therapy after membrane stabilisation. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol over 30 minutes to shift potassium intracellularly
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is reserved for severe metabolic acidosis with hyperkalaemia and is not the first-line membrane stabiliser. (Source: KHA-CARI)

options.text: Administer intravenous calcium gluconate 10 percent 10 mL over 2-5 minutes to stabilise the myocardial membrane
options.rationale: With ECG changes (peaked T, widened QRS, loss of P waves) and K above 6.0 mmol/L, immediate IV calcium stabilises the myocardial membrane within 1-3 minutes (lasts 30-60 minutes), buying time for K-shifting and elimination therapies. (Source: KHA-CARI; ANZCOR)

options.text: Administer sodium polystyrene sulfonate 30 g orally as the most effective first measure for total body potassium
options.rationale: Polystyrene sulfonate has slow onset (hours), questionable efficacy, and risk of bowel necrosis; not for acute severe hyperkalaemia. (Source: AMH; TGA Safety Alert)

explanation.summary: Severe hyperkalaemia (K above 6.0 mmol/L) with ECG changes is a medical emergency. Management has three phases: membrane stabilisation (IV calcium gluconate 10 percent 10 mL over 2-5 min, immediate effect, lasts 30-60 min, repeat if ECG changes persist), potassium shift (insulin plus dextrose, salbutamol, bicarbonate if acidotic), and elimination (loop diuretic, dialysis if oligoanuric or refractory). Identify and reverse the trigger: ACE inhibitors, MR antagonists, NSAIDs, trimethoprim, beta-blockers, renal failure, tumour lysis. In this patient, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is the likely added precipitant.
key_points: K above 6.0 with ECG changes = emergency
Membrane stabilisation FIRST: IV calcium gluconate 10 percent 10 mL
Then K-shift: insulin plus dextrose, salbutamol
Identify trigger (trimethoprim, ACEi, K-sparing diuretic, AKI)
explanation.pearls: Trimethoprim blocks the renal epithelial sodium channel similar to amiloride and can raise serum K by 1-2 mmol/L within days of starting in CKD patients on ACEi or spironolactone - always check K within 7-10 days of any new trimethoprim prescription in CKD.

subtopic_detail: Immediate antibiotic threshold in bilateral acute OM under 2 years
stem: A 15-month-old boy presents to your GP clinic with 24 hours of fever, irritability and right ear-tugging. He has no significant past medical history, is fully immunised, and is non-Aboriginal. He has no allergies. On examination he has a temperature of 38.7, both tympanic membranes bulging with reduced light reflex, and red middle ear effusion bilaterally. There is no mastoid tenderness and no facial weakness. He is alert and drinking.

data_table.Examination: Bilateral bulging red tympanic membranes; no mastoid tenderness; no neck stiffness; well perfused; drinking 60 percent of usual

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure parents, advise paracetamol or ibuprofen for analgesia, and review in 48 hours if symptoms persist
options.rationale: Watchful waiting is appropriate for non-severe unilateral otitis media in children over 2 years with mild symptoms; under 2 years with bilateral disease meets the threshold for immediate antibiotics. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Otitis Media)

options.text: Prescribe topical ciprofloxacin ear drops twice daily for 5 days as first-line treatment for acute otitis media
options.rationale: Topical antibiotics are for otitis externa or chronic discharging ears, not acute otitis media with intact tympanic membrane; oral amoxicillin is first-line. (Source: RCH CPG)

options.text: Prescribe amoxicillin 30 mg/kg every 8 hours orally for 5 days plus regular paracetamol or ibuprofen analgesia
options.rationale: AU guidelines (RCH, eTG) recommend immediate oral amoxicillin in children under 2 years with bilateral acute otitis media, severe symptoms (severe pain, T 39 or above, vomiting), or systemic features. Dose 30 mg/kg 8-hourly for 5 days. Analgesia is essential. (Source: RCH CPG; eTG)

options.text: Prescribe cefuroxime 15 mg/kg twice daily orally for 7 days as the first-line agent in childhood otitis media
options.rationale: Cefuroxime is reserved for amoxicillin failure or non-severe penicillin allergy; amoxicillin is first-line. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Refer to ENT for urgent tympanostomy tube placement given the bilateral nature of his presentation today
options.rationale: Tympanostomy referral is for recurrent (3 or more in 6 months or 4 or more in 12 months) or chronic OM with effusion lasting over 3 months, not a single acute episode. (Source: RCH CPG)

explanation.summary: Acute otitis media in children is largely viral; bacterial cases involve S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis. Immediate antibiotics in Australia are reserved for under 6 months, under 2 years with bilateral disease, severe systemic features (T 39 or above, severe pain, vomiting), suppurative complications, or Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children at higher risk of complications. Otherwise watchful waiting 48 hours with safety-netting. Amoxicillin 30 mg/kg 8-hourly for 5 days is first-line. Augmentin reserved for treatment failure or recurrent. Analgesia is mandatory regardless of antibiotic decision.
key_points: Watchful wait OR immediate amoxicillin based on age, severity, risk group
Under 2 with bilateral disease = treat immediately
Amoxicillin 30 mg/kg 8-hourly for 5 days
Analgesia regardless of antibiotic decision
explanation.pearls: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children with acute otitis media have an 8-10 times higher rate of progression to chronic suppurative otitis media and hearing loss, so eTG recommends immediate antibiotic treatment for all acute OM in this population regardless of age or severity.

subtopic_detail: Supportive management thresholds in infant bronchiolitis
stem: A 10-month-old boy presents to your ED in late autumn with 3 days of coryza, rhinorrhoea, mild fever and 24 hours of wheezing and cough. His older sister had a cold last week. He has no prior wheeze, no atopy in the family, and is breastfed plus solid food. He is fully immunised. He has had no prior salbutamol use. He has 30 percent reduced feeding.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8, HR 140, RR 50, SpO2 95 percent RA
data_table.Examination: Mild subcostal recession, widespread wheeze and bilateral fine crackles, mild dehydration with capillary refill 2 seconds; alert

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Trial salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer with reassessment of respiratory effort and oxygen saturation at 20 minutes
options.rationale: Salbutamol is not effective in children under 12 months with bronchiolitis (no smooth muscle to act on, no benefit shown in trials); RCH recommends against routine use. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Supportive care with feeds and oxygen monitoring; admit if SpO2 below 92 percent on room air or feeds below 50 percent of normal
options.rationale: Bronchiolitis in infants is a supportive management diagnosis. RCH CPG recommends nasal saline, oxygen if SpO2 below 92 percent, NG or IV fluids if oral intake below 50 percent, and no routine salbutamol, steroids, adrenaline or antibiotics. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal cannula oxygen at 2 L/kg/min as first-line therapy without trialling standard low-flow oxygen
options.rationale: HFNC is escalation for moderate-severe distress not maintained on standard low-flow; not routine first-line. (Source: RCH CPG)

options.text: Administer oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 3 days to reduce airway inflammation and shorten time to discharge
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are not effective in bronchiolitis (no benefit shown in multiple large RCTs). (Source: RCH CPG; Cochrane)

options.text: Administer nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 strength with continuous monitoring for 4 hours before reassessment
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline shows no benefit in routine bronchiolitis and may cause rebound; reserved for severe cases with failure of supportive measures. (Source: RCH CPG)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis in children under 12 months with viral lower respiratory tract symptoms. RSV is the commonest cause (60-70 percent). Management is supportive: gentle nasal saline, minimal handling, oxygen if SpO2 below 92 percent, fluids via NG or IV if oral intake below 50 percent normal, and apnoea monitoring in younger infants. Salbutamol, corticosteroids, adrenaline, hypertonic saline, and antibiotics are NOT routinely indicated. Escalation criteria: persistent hypoxia despite oxygen, exhaustion, recurrent apnoeas, severe distress.
key_points: Supportive care only - oxygen, feeds, observation
No salbutamol, no steroids, no adrenaline routinely
Admission if SpO2 below 92, feeds below 50 percent, apnoeas, severe distress
Antibiotics only if secondary bacterial infection
explanation.pearls: Apnoeas occur most commonly in infants under 6 weeks or preterm with bronchiolitis and may be the presenting feature even before respiratory distress is severe; cardiopulmonary monitoring is mandatory in this group regardless of initial SpO2.

stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to your GP clinic by his mother with 6 weeks of infrequent painful stools and recent soiling episodes. He passes a hard stool every 4-5 days and reports pain on defaecation. He is otherwise well, eats a variable diet, drinks plenty of water, and has not had recent illness. Growth is along the 50th centile, immunisations up to date. He has no red flags such as delayed meconium passage, blood per rectum, or anorexia.

data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen with palpable faecal mass in the left lower quadrant; perianal inspection normal; no anal fissure visible; growth on 50th centile

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence Senna 5 mg nocte as a stimulant laxative without an initial disimpaction phase before maintenance
options.rationale: Stimulant laxatives can cause cramping pain and are second-line; osmotic laxative (PEG) is first-line in AU. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Order plain abdominal X-ray to confirm faecal loading and refer to paediatric gastroenterology outpatients
options.rationale: AXR is not routinely required; clinical diagnosis is reliable; specialist referral is reserved for red flags or failure of maintenance therapy. (Source: RCH CPG)

options.text: Refer to paediatric surgery for digital rectal examination under general anaesthetic for manual disimpaction
options.rationale: Manual disimpaction under GA is reserved for refractory cases failing oral disimpaction; oral PEG is first-line and effective in around 90 percent. (Source: RCH CPG)

options.text: Commence macrogol 3350 with electrolytes at disimpaction dose for 3-7 days then maintenance dose
options.rationale: Macrogol 3350 with electrolytes (Movicol Junior) is the first-line osmotic laxative in Australia for childhood functional constipation; disimpaction dose 1-1.5 sachets/kg/day for 3-7 days, then transition to maintenance 0.4-1 sachets/day adjusted to soft daily stool. (Source: RCH CPG; AMH)

options.text: Recommend dietary fibre, water and timed toileting routine alone with review of progress in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Lifestyle alone is insufficient when faecal impaction has occurred; impaction must be cleared before maintenance succeeds. (Source: RCH CPG)

explanation.summary: Functional constipation accounts for over 95 percent of paediatric constipation. Red flags requiring further workup include delayed meconium passage beyond 48 hours of life, growth failure, abdominal distension, vomiting, blood per rectum, abnormal neurological signs, or sacral dimple. Standard care involves two phases: disimpaction (oral macrogol 3350 with electrolytes 1-1.5 sachets/kg/day for 3-7 days), then maintenance (PEG titrated to one soft stool daily, minimum 3-6 months). Lactulose is second-line. Behavioural training including timed toileting after meals and a stool diary support adherence.
key_points: Disimpaction THEN maintenance - both phases necessary
Macrogol 3350 with electrolytes (PEG) is first-line in Australia
Maintenance for minimum 3-6 months, often longer
Behavioural toileting routine integral to success
explanation.pearls: Approximately 50 percent of children with functional constipation relapse within 1 year of stopping maintenance laxatives, so plan a gradual wean over 2-3 months only after 6 months of soft daily stools without soiling; abrupt cessation predictably triggers re-impaction.

subtopic_detail: Toddler iron deficiency anaemia with cow's milk excess
stem: A 22-month-old girl is brought to your GP clinic for review of pallor. Her mother reports she drinks 750 mL of cow's milk per day from a bottle, eats variably, and refuses red meat. She is otherwise well, walks normally, and has met all developmental milestones. She is fully immunised. She has 5 episodes per day of upset and is described as fussy by her mother.

data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae, no koilonychia, no hepatosplenomegaly, weight on 50th centile, height on 50th centile
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92 g/L, MCV 64 fL, ferritin 7 mcg/L, transferrin saturation 6 percent, CRP under 5; coeliac serology negative; blood film microcytic hypochromic; reticulocyte count 1.8 percent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassure that the anaemia is mild and continue current diet with a routine review in 4 months
options.rationale: Iron deficiency anaemia at 22 months requires active treatment and dietary modification; untreated risks cognitive and developmental impact. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency Anaemia)

options.text: Order serum lead level and refer urgently to paediatric haematology for further workup before treatment
options.rationale: Lead toxicity is rare in Australian children without risk factors; not standard initial workup. Iron deficiency is the typical cause of microcytic anaemia in this age. (Source: RCH CPG)

options.text: Commence vitamin B12 1000 micrograms intramuscularly weekly for 4 weeks then 3-monthly maintenance
options.rationale: B12 deficiency causes macrocytic not microcytic anaemia; the FBC pattern points to iron deficiency. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Prescribe oral ferrous fumarate 100 mg daily without dietary advice or milk reduction during this visit
options.rationale: Iron supplementation without dietary modification is incomplete; cow's milk excess (over 500 mL/day) is a major driver and must be addressed. (Source: RCH CPG)

options.text: Prescribe oral elemental iron 3 mg/kg daily, limit cow's milk to 500 mL/day, advise iron-rich foods, recheck in 8 weeks
options.rationale: RCH guidelines: oral elemental iron 3 mg/kg/day for 3 months, reduce cow's milk to below 500 mL/day, encourage iron-rich foods (red meat, fortified cereals, legumes), and recheck at 8 weeks expecting Hb rise of 10 g/L or more. (Source: RCH CPG)

explanation.summary: Iron deficiency anaemia affects 8-10 percent of Australian toddlers; risk factors include excessive cow's milk (over 500 mL/day), early cow's milk introduction before 12 months, low iron-containing solids, prolonged breastfeeding without iron supplementation, prematurity, and Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander background. Diagnostic markers: microcytic hypochromic anaemia, low ferritin (below 12 mcg/L), low transferrin saturation. Treatment: oral elemental iron 3 mg/kg/day for 3 months minimum, reduce cow's milk to below 500 mL/day, dietary modification, FBC and ferritin at 8 weeks. Iron deficiency without anaemia (low ferritin alone) still warrants treatment given developmental risk.
key_points: Most common cause of microcytic anaemia in toddlers
Cow's milk excess is the main modifiable factor
Oral iron 3 mg/kg/day elemental for 3 months
Recheck FBC at 8 weeks; Hb rise of 10 g/L or more confirms adequate response
explanation.pearls: Iron deficiency in the second year of life is associated with measurable IQ deficits up to 10 points and impaired motor development that may persist even after iron repletion; the window for prevention is more important than the rapidity of correction.

subtopic_detail: Empiric IV antibiotic regimen in febrile infant under 3 months
stem: A 2-month-old infant is brought to your ED with 24 hours of fever (38.6 by parents), poor feeding, irritability, and a single episode of vomiting. He has no upper respiratory or other localising symptoms. He is fully immunised for age. On examination he is alert but irritable, with a temperature of 38.7, capillary refill 2 seconds, no rash, no meningism, and clear chest. His urine is collected by clean catch.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7, HR 165, RR 50, SpO2 99 percent RA, weight 5.2 kg
data_table.Examination: Alert but irritable; no rash; anterior fontanelle soft; chest clear; no abdominal tenderness; perfusion adequate
data_table.Investigations: Bedside urine dipstick positive for leukocytes and nitrites; FBC, CRP, blood cultures, blood gas pending; LP being arranged

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic management?

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin empiric coverage following blood and urine cultures and lumbar puncture
options.rationale: In infants under 3 months with fever, the threshold for serious bacterial infection workup and empiric antibiotic cover is low; sepsis cannot be excluded clinically. The RCH approach is full septic screen (blood culture, urine catheter or SPA, LP) and empiric IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin, covering E. coli, Listeria, Group B strep, and other neonatal pathogens. (Source: RCH CPG; eTG)

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg/dose four times daily for 7 days as outpatient management with GP follow-up
options.rationale: Oral therapy is inappropriate in febrile infants under 3 months; sepsis evaluation and IV therapy are mandatory. (Source: RCH CPG)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone monotherapy at 50 mg/kg once daily without need for second agent
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone alone covers most enterics but does not reliably cover Listeria, which is important in this age group; AU empiric regimen includes benzylpenicillin or ampicillin for Listeria. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole orally as suitable empiric treatment for outpatient management of UTI
options.rationale: Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is inappropriate for septic febrile infant; risks delayed source control and bilirubin displacement in neonates. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until urine culture is back at 48 hours and reassess clinically before commencing therapy
options.rationale: Withholding antibiotics in a febrile young infant is dangerous; outcomes worsen markedly with each hour of delay. (Source: RCH CPG)

explanation.summary: Fever in infants under 3 months requires systematic evaluation because clinical signs are unreliable for identifying serious bacterial infection (SBI, around 10 percent prevalence). AU practice: full septic screen (blood cultures, urine via catheter or suprapubic aspirate, LP) and empiric IV antibiotics. Standard regimen IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin (or amoxicillin plus gentamicin) covers E. coli, Group B Streptococcus, Listeria monocytogenes, and Streptococcus pneumoniae. Add aciclovir if HSV suspected. Admit for at least 48 hours pending culture results.
key_points: Fever under 3 months - full septic screen plus empiric IV antibiotics
Empiric: benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin (covers Listeria plus neonatal pathogens)
Add aciclovir if HSV concern (vesicles, seizures, hepatitis)
Admit minimum 48 hours
explanation.pearls: Suprapubic aspirate is the gold standard urine collection in infants under 6 months when clean-catch is not feasible because bag samples have around a 25 percent contamination rate; ultrasound-guided SPA increases first-pass success to around 95 percent.

subtopic_detail: IVIG and aspirin for classic Kawasaki disease
stem: A 4-year-old boy presents to your ED on day 6 of high spiking fevers up to 40, accompanied by a polymorphous rash on day 2, bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis on day 3, a strawberry tongue with red cracked lips on day 4, and unilateral right cervical lymphadenopathy 2.5 cm. His feet became swollen yesterday with red palms. He is irritable and lethargic. He has been seen twice by his GP for viral illness.

data_table.Examination: Bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis; cracked red lips and strawberry tongue; polymorphous rash trunk and limbs; right cervical lymphadenopathy 2.5 cm; oedema and erythema of palms and soles; no signs of meningism
data_table.Investigations: FBC WCC 18, neutrophils 13, platelets 580; CRP 198; ESR 78; Na 132; albumin 28; ALT 78; urinary sediment with 15 WCC; echocardiogram pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence amoxicillin and observe for clinical response over 48 hours before further investigations are arranged
options.rationale: This presentation meets full Kawasaki disease criteria (fever over 5 days plus four of five principal features); antibiotics alone risk progression to coronary artery aneurysms. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Treat as scarlet fever with oral phenoxymethylpenicillin and observe response over the next 72 hours
options.rationale: Scarlet fever does not typically include conjunctivitis or extremity changes; testing for Kawasaki and treating now is essential. (Source: RCH CPG)

options.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg with high-dose aspirin and arrange urgent echocardiogram
options.rationale: Kawasaki disease (fever 5 or more days plus 4 of 5: bilateral conjunctivitis, oral changes, polymorphous rash, peripheral extremity changes, cervical lymphadenopathy) requires IVIG 2 g/kg over 10-12 hours within 10 days of fever onset to reduce coronary aneurysm risk from 25 percent to under 5 percent; combined with aspirin 30-50 mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses until afebrile, then 3-5 mg/kg/day until echo confirms no aneurysm. (Source: RCH CPG; AHA 2017)

options.text: Administer intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg as monotherapy without immunoglobulin or aspirin
options.rationale: Steroid monotherapy is reserved for IVIG-resistant cases as second-line; not first-line in Australia. (Source: RCH CPG)

options.text: Reassure parents of viral exanthem, advise paracetamol, and arrange GP review of progress in 48 hours
options.rationale: Failure to recognise and treat KD risks coronary aneurysms; this child has multiple criteria for KD. (Source: RCH CPG)

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease is a self-limited medium-vessel vasculitis of young children (peak age under 5) with major sequelae being coronary artery aneurysms in 25 percent of untreated cases. Diagnostic criteria: fever 5 or more days PLUS 4 or more of: bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, oral changes (strawberry tongue, red cracked lips), polymorphous rash, peripheral extremity changes, cervical lymphadenopathy. Atypical or incomplete KD requires lab criteria (CRP, ESR, anaemia, platelets over 450, hypoalbuminaemia, sterile pyuria). Treatment: IVIG 2 g/kg single dose within 10 days reduces aneurysm risk to under 5 percent, plus high-dose aspirin transitioning to low-dose. Echocardiogram at baseline, 2 weeks and 6-8 weeks.
key_points: 5 day fever plus 4 of 5 criteria = classic Kawasaki
IVIG 2 g/kg single infusion within 10 days
Aspirin high-dose acutely, then low-dose for 6-8 weeks pending echo
Echo at baseline, 2 weeks, 6-8 weeks
explanation.pearls: Incomplete or atypical Kawasaki disease should be considered in any child under 6 months or over 5 years with 5 or more days of unexplained fever; coronary aneurysm risk is paradoxically highest in infants under 6 months despite often having fewer classic clinical criteria.
